steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content:
The sentence contains two independent clauses: the first ends with the quote about producing “the most outstanding work in an idealistic direction” (stating the prize criterion per Alfred Nobel); the second begins with “In 1912, for instance, judges recognized Gerhart Johann Robert Hauptmann...” (providing a concrete example).

These are closely related—the second illustrates the first.

steps.title: Apply the rule for separating independent clauses
steps.content:
A semicolon joins two independent clauses with a close relationship.

In American English, when a semicolon is not part of the quoted material itself, it goes outside the closing quotation mark.

Choice C correctly positions it: direction; connects the two clauses smoothly.

A fails to separate them, B awkwardly inserts “and,” disrupting the flow, and D places a comma inside the quote—which neither properly separates independent clauses nor follows the rule that semicolons must go outside quotes.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
